title: More Sorrows
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Review: The Four Scenes of Job Chapter 1
subpoints: Scene 1: Job the hero — a blameless, upright, rich man whom God himself commended
Scene 2: Satan the villain — bursting into heaven to accuse God’s worthiness of worship
Scene 3: The plot tension — God instigates the trial by pointing Satan to Job
Scene 4: Job’s response — grief, worship, and ‘the Lord gave and the Lord has taken away’

outline.title: God Is Sovereign Over Human Suffering
subpoints: God instigates the persecution that Satan brings — Job acknowledges this, not ‘Satan took it away’
God’s sovereignty over suffering is both a comfort and a challenge to faith
The sparrow does not fall apart from the Father’s will — nothing comes to us outside God’s ordaining
Without a sovereign God, suffering has no purpose; with him, even the darkest trial has meaning

outline.title: Satan’s Accusation Should Lead Us to Examine Our Hearts
subpoints: Satan’s contention: God is not worthy of worship unless he blesses us
The real question: would you fear God, worship God, love God even if there were no blessings?
Are we drawn to the giver or to the gifts?
If all that were left were Jesus Christ, would we continue to worship God?

outline.title: Why We Suffer: Job Was Allowed to Suffer to Demonstrate the Genuineness of His Faith
subpoints: 1 Peter 1:6-7: trials test faith — and faith found genuine is of great worth at the revelation of Christ
Job was also allowed to suffer to glorify God — showing that God is worthy of worship for who he is, not for his gifts
John 9:3: the man born blind suffered that the works of God might be revealed in him
The cross of Christ is the ultimate example of suffering turned into the greatest revelation of God’s glory

outline.title: What Does It Look Like to Suffer Well?
subpoints: Grief, sorrow, and mourning are part of suffering well — not signs of failure
Worshiping God in the suffering and recognizing his sovereignty from the heart
Acknowledging that God is still good in the midst of loss: ‘blessed be the name of the Lord’
Looking to Jesus: who for the joy set before him endured the cross (Hebrews 12:1-3)

summary:
This Sunday School lesson continues the verse-by-verse study of Job 1, completing the examination of chapter 1 and drawing three major practical lessons for the congregation. The session opens with a brief review of the four scenes studied last week: Job the righteous hero, Satan the accuser, the plot tension introduced by God’s own pointing of Satan toward Job, and Job’s initial grief-filled but faithful response.

The teacher then develops three lessons from the chapter. The first is that God is sovereign over human suffering. Job does not say ‘Satan took it away’ but ‘the Lord gave and the Lord has taken away.’ He acknowledges the ultimate controller. This reality is both a comfort and a challenge: comforting because nothing falls outside God’s purposeful hand, and challenging because it raises the hardest question of the Christian faith — why does God allow evil and suffering to exist? The teacher does not attempt a full answer but establishes what Scripture affirms: God is not the origin of evil (there is no darkness in him), and yet he ordains and governs it. Without sovereignty, suffering is meaningless; with it, even the darkest trial has a purpose.

The second lesson is that Satan’s accusation should drive us to examine our hearts. Satan’s contention is not really about Job — it is a claim that God is not worthy of worship for who he is, only for what he gives. This forces the personal question: would we still fear God, worship God, love God if all earthly blessings were stripped away? If Christ alone were left, would that be enough?

The third lesson is that suffering can be purposeful: Job was allowed to suffer to prove the genuineness of his faith and to glorify God. Peter makes this explicit in 1 Peter 1:6-7. John 9:3 offers a New Testament parallel — the man was born blind so that the works of God might be revealed in him. And the cross of Christ is the supreme instance: the ancient instrument of shame became the greatest revelation of God’s glory in all history. The session closes with a discussion of what it means to suffer well, which includes genuine grief, honest lament, active worship, acknowledgment of God’s sovereignty, and looking to Jesus who for the joy set before him endured the cross.
transcript:
## Introduction

Job played an important role in a Tom Cruise movie. Anybody know which movie that was? Right, Mission: Impossible. The key to break the code is in Job 3:14. He’s got a staircase. There’s a Bible on the bookshelf. He grabs it. Who knew Tom Cruise knew about the Bible?

Linda asked about the sons of God in last week’s passage. The sons of God listed in Job 1:6 are the angels in this particular passage. There are a couple of places where that expression is used to refer to angels. And it’s interesting that it can also refer to fallen angels as well. That is a title for angels, used in the plural in these situations.

Now remember Claudius’s line from last week that he said, when sorrows come, they come not single spies but in battalions. The idea that when it rains it pours. And that’s exactly what is going on in chapters 1 and 2. Job’s life is falling apart, and it’s falling apart at God’s instigation. That’s something that we can’t miss. Look again at verse 8: then the Lord said to Satan, have you considered my servant Job, that there is none like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man, one who fears God and shuns evil. That is God who instigates the persecution that Satan brings into Job’s life.

There are at least three lessons for us in this chapter that we will take a look at toward the end of our time. But as we begin, let’s review what we saw last week.

## Review of the Four Scenes of Job Chapter 1

We saw that we can divide the first chapter into four scenes, and we took a look at the first two scenes of the book of Job. The first one being where we meet our hero and our hero is Job. Look at verses 1 through 5: there was a man in the land of Uz whose name was Job and that man was blameless and upright and one who feared God and shunned evil. In seven sons and three daughters were born to him. Also his possessions were 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of ox and 500 female donkeys and a very large household so that this man was the greatest of all the people of the east.

So Job was a pious man. He wanted his family to be right with the Lord. He wanted to intercede and minister on behalf of his family. He was an upright man, a man who followed the Lord, of great wealth, and well thought of in society.

But we also met our villain last week — in the book of Job the one who wears the black hats is Satan. Look at verse 6: now there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord and Satan also came among them. And the Lord said to Satan, from where do you come? So Satan answered the Lord and said, from going to and fro on the earth and from walking back and forth on it. Then the Lord said to Satan, have you considered my servant Job, that there is none like him on the earth? A blameless and upright man, one who fears God and shuns evil. So Satan answered the Lord and said, does Job fear God for nothing?

So here we have the villain come in, and the tension introduced that’s going to be explored for the rest of the book. Satan is not really just attacking Job. Job is just a pawn in the game that he is playing. What he’s really saying is: God, you are not worthy of being worshiped unless you give all this kind of gifts to people. If Job didn’t have all these gifts, he will not worship you, because you God are not worthy of worship. That’s what Satan is doing — accusing God.

Those are the first two scenes that we saw last week. Today we are going to start with the third scene in the first chapter of Job and the plot tension where the issues that’s going to dominate the rest of the book is going to be described. We find that in verses 12 through 19.

## Scene 3: The Plot Tension — The Battalion of Sorrows

It is important that we notice that the text makes it crystal clear that God was fully in charge here. God is not just a side player. God is fully in charge of everything that is going on here. Look at verse 12: and the Lord said to Satan, behold, all that he has is in your power. Only do not lay a hand on his person. So Satan went out from the presence of the Lord. Satan can only do what God allows him to do. God is always in full control.

What unfolds in the remainder of the first chapter is an account of a series of brutal attacks on Job’s blessings. Remember what Satan’s thesis is: remove the blessings and Job is not going to worship you, because God just being God is not worthy of his worship. In chapter 1, God says, he can’t touch him, only his things and his family. But not him personally — that comes in chapter 2.

And these attacks come in very rapid succession, stacked upon each other, with each successive attack growing in intensity and brutality. Look at verses 14 through 15: a messenger came to Job and said, the oxen were plowing and the donkeys were feeding beside them when the Sabeans raided them and took them away. Indeed they have killed the servants with the edge of the sword and I alone have escaped to tell you. The Sabeans killed all Job’s servants and took all his oxen and donkeys, and leave just one to tell the news. That’s the theme here.

And then while he was still speaking, another also came and said, the fire of God fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and the servants and consumed them, and I alone have escaped to tell you. And even as he’s still talking, Job loses all his camels as the Chaldeans raid Job’s flock. Look at verse 17: while he was still speaking, another also came and said, the Chaldeans formed three bands, raided the camels and took them away, and killed the servants with the edge of the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you.

Satan made sure that the bad news would get to Job. You can notice the repetition: while he was still speaking. The increased intensity of these attacks is revealed in the fact that each successive loss is more valuable than the previous one. In the ancient Near East, sheep were more valuable than oxen and donkeys — that’s the second attack. The first attack was the oxen and the second attack was the sheep, and then the camels were the most precious of all the animals. And that was the third attack.

And then the ultimate attack was when Satan killed all his kids. Look at verses 18 and 19: while he was still speaking, another also came and said, your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother’s house, and suddenly a great wind came from across the wilderness and struck the four corners of the house, and it fell on the young people and they are dead, and I alone have escaped to tell you. All ten kids dead. All at once in one evening they are gone. To lose one child is overwhelming. But to lose all your children all at once — that’s just unimaginable.

And the plot tension is found in how Job will respond to all his losses. Will Job maintain his integrity and vindicate himself and by implication vindicate God? Or will Job curse God and prove Satan correct?

## Scene 4: Job’s Response — Partial Resolution

That’s where scene four comes in with the resolution — this is a partial resolution because the plot will continue throughout the book. Look at verses 20 through 22: then Job arose, tore his robe and shaved his head, and he fell to the ground and worshiped. And he said, naked I came from my mother’s womb and naked shall I return there. The Lord gave and the Lord has taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord. In all this Job did not sin or charge God with wrong.

Had the book stopped here, if this were the end of it, then God would not have rebuked Job as he does at the end of the book. Because as far as we know so far, Job has been righteous in the sight of God, as verse 22 tells us.

How does Job respond to God? We see first that he responds with deep, profound grief. He tore his clothes and shaved his head. This is the indication of sorrow and grief in his heart. And yet Job maintained his integrity and demonstrated by his actions that God is worthy of worship for who he is, and not merely for the blessings that he gives. You see the pain in verse 21, you see the faith there in verse 21.

And notice that contentment doesn’t have to be this happy-clappy thing. You think Job was singing this with a very major-key harmony and happiness? No, this would be a dirge, a minor-key song if you were singing it. He was content with the sovereignty of God and the blessings that God had given him for the time that God had given him. The Lord gave it and Job was happy that he had had his children, his possessions. And the Lord took it away. He continues to bless the name of the Lord.

## Three Lessons for Us from Chapter 1

Now these are not going to be earth-shattering or novel. I don’t come up with new ideas — I borrow from other people and put them together. But these are things that are good for us to hear again and again.

### Lesson 1: God Is Sovereign Over Human Suffering

This is important for us to hear and to hear again, so that when we go through suffering we have a firm grasp on this. God is sovereign over human suffering. After Job experiences the series of tragic events that we just saw, he falls to the ground and worships God. He doesn’t say ‘Satan did all this to me,’ which would have been true. But he acknowledges the ultimate controller, the sovereign over all things. And notice that Job doesn’t say ‘the Lord gave and Satan took it away.’ He says, the Lord gave and the Lord has taken away.

This reality is both a comfort and a challenge to our faith. Knowing that God is fully sovereign over all that comes to pass in this world and this life is tremendously comforting. There’s great comfort in knowing that God is in charge and that nothing can happen that God has not purposefully ordained. As Jesus says, are not two sparrows sold for a copper coin, and not one of them falls to the ground apart from your Father’s will? But the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Do not fear therefore; you are of more value than many sparrows.

Knowing that God is in charge of everything that happens to us, and that he loves us, allows us to get through some very difficult situations. The two most difficult situations that I can conceive of in a human experience are these: the most difficult would be the loss of a child going astray spiritually. I think that’s worse than the death of a child. And second would be the loss of a child to death. But to go through that without the assurance that a God who loves me is in control and appointed that very thing to happen — it would be devastating, because that means that those things have no purpose.

We have some friends who years ago lost a son to brain cancer at 18 years old. And yes, why? And if God is not in charge of that, then that boy died for no reason whatsoever. He suffered great suffering for no reason whatsoever. But if God is in control and sovereign over all things, then we have hope that there’s a purpose in what’s going on.

But at the same time, as encouraging as it is to know that God is in charge of suffering, knowing that God is in charge of everything that happens to us and that he is in charge of suffering raises the question: why does God allow evil and suffering to exist? And that’s the greatest challenge to the Christian faith. Not the Trinity, not anything else — why does God allow suffering to exist? That is the major challenge.

We know that God is not the origin of evil. James tells us: let no one say when he is tempted, I am tempted by God, for God cannot be tempted by evil, nor does he himself tempt anyone. And 1 Corinthians 14:33 says, for God is not the author of confusion but of peace. And 1 John says, this is the message that we have heard from him and declare to you, that God is light and in him there is no darkness at all. So there’s no evil in God. Therefore he cannot be the author or the origin of evil. And yet he is in control of it. We also know from our text that God allowed Job to suffer. And whether we say allowed, permitted, or ordained — they all mean the same thing. One word might be more palatable for us than the other, but they all convey the same reality.

### Lesson 2: Satan’s Accusation Should Lead Us to Examine Our Hearts

Lesson number two: Satan’s accusation should lead us to examine our hearts. What is Satan’s accusation? That God is not worthy of worship unless he blesses us. So we have to ask ourselves: would I fear God even if there were no blessings? Would I fear God, would I worship God, would I love God even if there were no blessings? The issue that Satan is bringing up is whether God deserves to be worshiped because of the greatness of his character, or must he buy his worship with gifts and promises of blessings.

So why do you worship God? Would you still worship him if you lost everything and he left you only with Christ? So Satan in accusing God actually helps us think about our own worship of God. Why is it that I worship God? Is it because of the blessings he gives, or because of the giver of the blessings? If all that were left were Jesus Christ, would we continue to worship God?

### Lesson 3: Why We Suffer — At Least Two Reasons

Lesson number three: why do we suffer? I said I’m not going to give full answers, and I’m still not going to. This is a partial answer that you already know what I’m going to say. But one thing we know for sure about Job’s suffering: Job was allowed to suffer in order to demonstrate the genuineness of his faith. He’s appointed to suffer so that his faith could be proven to be true, honest, genuine, real.

And the Bible tells us that’s the same with us. First Peter 1:6-7: in this you greatly rejoice, though now for a little while if need be you have been grieved by various trials. Why? So that in verse 7, the genuineness of your faith, being much more precious than gold that perishes, though it be tested by fire, may be found to praise, honor, and glory at the revelation of Jesus Christ.

Job was also allowed to suffer in order to glorify God. Everything that’s done is done for the glory of God, and Job was allowed to suffer to glorify God. In allowing Job to suffer, God was not only vindicating Job, but he was also showing his worthiness to be worshiped simply for being God and not because of the blessings he gives to his people.

This is not the only story in the Bible where somebody suffers in order to glorify God. Can you think of another story that is clearly stated: this is happening so that God can be glorified? Jesus addresses this directly in John chapter 9, where his disciples ask him, Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind? The same assumption that Job was dealing with. And Jesus says, neither this man nor his parents sinned, but that the works of God should be revealed in him. Have you ever thought about suffering as an opportunity to glorify God? Have you ever thought about something you’re going through as a God-given opportunity to glorify him?

The cross of Jesus Christ, which was an ancient instrument of suffering and shame, became for Jesus a means of revealing the glory of God. That’s the ultimate turning of suffering into glory: the cross of Jesus Christ, where the glory of God was revealed on the cross.

## What Does It Mean to Suffer Well?

[Discussion with congregation about what suffering well looks like, prompted by a question about Job.]

Sorrow is part of suffering well. Grief is part of suffering well. Worshiping God is part of suffering well. Mourning is part of suffering well. Recognizing God’s sovereignty — not in a detached and cold way but actually from the heart, acknowledging that God is the one who brought this into your life — that is suffering well. Not trying to excuse God from the situation is suffering well. And acknowledging that God is still good in the midst of loss — ‘blessed be the name of the Lord’ — that is part of suffering well.

If you look at 1 Peter, an increase in faith through suffering is suffering well. Having faith that is greater and purified in suffering is suffering well. If you look at Joseph’s situation, being upright in your suffering is part of suffering well. Paul, in all that he kept giving glory to God — that is suffering well.

[Discussion: should we desire to suffer for Christ? The teacher answers: we should count it joy when suffering comes, as James 1 says, but desiring suffering per se is not what Scripture calls us to. We don’t pray for persecution, but if it comes, we pray for the faith to endure it well. The example of Ignatius of Antioch — who longed to be martyred by being eaten by beasts — is mentioned as perhaps going further than Scripture intends.]

## Conclusion

At the end of the day, suffering is a complex subject. It’s not something that is black and white all the way through, and there are no easy answers. And the answers become even more difficult in the middle of the suffering. That’s why we’re trying to do this when there are no major things going on that we’re going through right now.

God does provide us with some answers to the mystery of human suffering in this chapter. Ultimately we need to trust that God has a good reason for allowing human suffering to exist. One of the ways we cultivate this trust in our hearts is by looking at the example of Jesus Christ. In Hebrews 12 it says, therefore we also, since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight and the sin which so easily ensnares us, and let us run with endurance the race that is set before us, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and has sat down at the right hand of the throne of God. For consider him who endured such hostility from sinners against himself, lest you become weary and discouraged in your souls.

Lord, I do not know why this is going on. I don’t know why you’re allowing me to suffer. But I know Jesus has suffered. He’s gone before us. And he stood through suffering, and I trust him, because here I am going to arrive at him as well. And as we journey through the book of Job, we will continually wrestle with the mysteries of human suffering. And I want, as we do that, to encourage you that when you feel doubt and confusion, you need to look to Jesus. Because Jesus really had no reason whatsoever to suffer. Nobody can say, I know, but he had that point zero zero one percent of badness in him that really deserved suffering. That’s not there. And yet he did, so that you and I could have eternal life.
